channel.title: Law Review Podcasts
channel.description: AI-narrated law review articles for on-the-go listening.
channel.itunes:author: Daniel Townsend

item.title: 04.07.26 Faculty Workshop - Cole
item.description: 04.07.26 Faculty Workshop - Cole
item.content:encoded:
04.07.26 Faculty Workshop - Cole

David Cole defends the First Amendment's core neutrality principle against progressive critics who argue that formal neutrality in an unequal world reinforces inequality. Drawing on his career trajectory from the Center for Constitutional Rights to the ACLU, Cole contends that empowering the government to restrict speech in the name of equality poses greater dangers than the speech itself.

Chapters:
0:08 Introduction
21:08 II. The Affirmative Case for Neutrality
1:01:00 III. Speech and Civil Rights
2:33:07 Conclusion (Day One)
2:36:34 Introduction
2:40:37 II. Was Free Speech Tamed?
2:50:39 III. Corporate Speech
3:01:23 IV. Commercial Speech and the New Lochner
3:23:49 V. Campaign Finance
3:44:33 VI. Donald Trump, Free Speech, and Civil Society
3:58:54 Conclusion — Can Free Speech Be Progressive?

item.title: Abraham & Sharkey - Untangling AI Liability
item.description: Kenneth S. Abraham & Catherine M. Sharkey — Untangling AI Liability
item.content:encoded:
Kenneth S. Abraham & Catherine M. Sharkey — Untangling AI Liability

Abraham and Sharkey provide a comprehensive analytical framework for applying tort law to harms caused by AI technologies. They argue that the "black-box" problem is less insurmountable than commonly assumed, showing that existing tort principles can address AI liability without requiring insight into AI's internal decision-making. The more significant challenge, they contend, is the overlooked jurisprudential question of whether AI liability rules should be uniform or diverse, arguing that a diverse, context-specific approach will prove optimal.

Chapters:
9:02 Introduction
24:01 I. AI as a Subject of Liability
35:45 II. Low-Hanging Fruit: AI and Conventional Liability Issues
1:17:29 III. The More Difficult Challenges: Diversity and Opacity
2:05:32 IV. Insurance
2:17:30 Conclusion

item.title: Cross - Tort Law and the American Economy
item.description: Frank B. Cross — Tort Law and the American Economy (Minnesota Law Review, 2011)
item.content:encoded:
Frank B. Cross — Tort Law and the American Economy (Minnesota Law Review, 2011)

Cross tests the widely asserted claim that tort law undermines American economic growth by examining state-level economic data against tort-law indices created by two pro-defendant organizations. Despite the common narrative that pro-plaintiff tort regimes discourage business investment and innovation, he finds no evidence that states with more defendant-friendly tort law perform better economically, suggesting that the economic case for tort reform has been significantly overstated.

Chapters:
2:40 I. The Controversy Over Tort Law
26:17 II. Research on Tort Law and the Economy
37:36 III. The Measures of Tort Law Used in This Study
51:18 IV. An Analysis of Economic Effects of State Tort Law
1:15:18 V. Pathways for Tort Law to Affect the General Economy

item.title: 6_Fiss_Forms_of_Justice_1979
item.description: Owen M. Fiss — The Forms of Justice (93 Harv. L. Rev. 1 (1979))
item.content:encoded:
Owen M. Fiss — The Forms of Justice (93 Harv. L. Rev. 1 (1979))

Owen Fiss's 1979 Harvard foreword makes the affirmative case for structural, public-law adjudication. Against the view that courts exist merely to resolve private disputes, Fiss argues that their distinctive function is to give concrete meaning to public values — the task exemplified by institutional-reform litigation over schools, prisons, and mental hospitals — and defends the judge's expansive remedial role in reconstructing organizations to conform to the Constitution.

Chapters:
9:18 I. Adjudication and Public Values
37:51 II. Form and Function
1:43:11 III. The Problem of Remedy

item.title: 10_Resnik_Failing_Faith_1986
item.description: Judith Resnik — Failing Faith: Adjudicatory Procedure in Decline (53 U. Chi. L. Rev. 494 (1986))
item.content:encoded:
Judith Resnik — Failing Faith: Adjudicatory Procedure in Decline (53 U. Chi. L. Rev. 494 (1986))

In this companion to Managerial Judges, Judith Resnik charts the decline of adjudication as the animating ideal of American civil procedure. She traces how the faith embodied in the 1938 Federal Rules — in trans-substantive procedure, liberal access, and the trial as the system's centerpiece — has eroded under caseload pressure, managerial judging, settlement, and privatized dispute resolution, and asks what is lost as courts turn away from adjudication.

Chapters:
24:46 II. From their effective date in 1938 until the early nineteen-seventies, the Federa

item.title: 8_Cover_For_James_Moore_1975
item.description: Robert M. Cover — For James Wm. Moore: Some Reflections on a Reading of the Rules (84 Yale L.J. 718 (1975))
item.content:encoded:
Robert M. Cover — For James Wm. Moore: Some Reflections on a Reading of the Rules (84 Yale L.J. 718 (1975))

Robert Cover's tribute to James Wm. Moore reflects on what it means to read the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure. He argues that procedure is never merely neutral or trans-substantive: the Rules embody contestable political and ideological commitments about the relationship between process and substance. A meditation on the jurisprudence of procedure and the values secreted in its interstices.

Chapters:
7:48 II. I accept the conclusion that procedure, broadly conceived, will in-
40:52 I. Case Co

item.title: 7_Mashaw_Due_Process_Calculus_1976
item.description: Jerry L. Mashaw — The Supreme Court's Due Process Calculus in Mathews v. Eldridge (44 U. Chi. L. Rev. 28 (1976))
item.content:encoded:
Jerry L. Mashaw — The Supreme Court's Due Process Calculus in Mathews v. Eldridge (44 U. Chi. L. Rev. 28 (1976))

Jerry Mashaw dissects the Supreme Court's three-factor due process test from Mathews v. Eldridge — weighing the private interest, the risk of error, and the government's administrative burden. He argues that this utilitarian calculus, for all its apparent rigor, rests on an impoverished theory of procedural value that slights the dignitary and participatory interests procedure also serves. A foundational critique of how American law decides how much process is due.

Chapters:
36:01 I. The Supreme Court's analysis in Eldridge is not informed by systematic attention

item.title: 2_Resnik_Managerial_Judges_1982
item.description: Judith Resnik — Managerial Judges (96 Harv. L. Rev. 374 (1982))
item.content:encoded:
Judith Resnik — Managerial Judges (96 Harv. L. Rev. 374 (1982))

Judith Resnik's foundational 1982 article documents how American trial judges moved beyond their traditional adjudicative role to become active managers of litigation — supervising pretrial preparation, pressing settlement, and overseeing decrees after judgment. She argues this managerial turn, conducted largely informally and off the record, spread with little scrutiny of its costs to judicial impartiality, due process, and public confidence in the courts.

Chapters:
0:38 Introduction Until
13:46 III. Managerial Judging: a Description
44:32 I. believe, without the benefit of much
1:02:06 IV. The Results of Pretrial Management
1:27:03 V. The Relevance of Robes
1:47:23 Conclusion

item.title: 9_Sander_Varieties_Dispute_Processing_1976
item.description: Frank E.A. Sander — Varieties of Dispute Processing (70 F.R.D. 111 (1976))
item.content:encoded:
Frank E.A. Sander — Varieties of Dispute Processing (70 F.R.D. 111 (1976))

Frank Sander's 1976 Pound Conference address launched the modern alternative-dispute-resolution movement and the idea of the multi-door courthouse. Surveying the varieties of dispute processing — mediation, arbitration, fact-finding, ombudsmen, and more — he proposes matching each kind of dispute to the forum best suited to resolve it, rather than defaulting every conflict to adjudication.

Chapters:
44:24 Conclusion

item.title: 93YaleLJ
item.description: Owen M. Fiss — Against Settlement (93 Yale L.J. 1073, 1984)
item.content:encoded:
Owen M. Fiss — Against Settlement (93 Yale L.J. 1073, 1984)

Owen Fiss's classic 1984 critique of the Alternative Dispute Resolution movement. Against the prevailing view that settlement is a desirable, cheaper substitute for adjudication, Fiss argues that settlement is the civil analogue of plea bargaining — a capitulation to the conditions of mass society that should be neither encouraged nor praised. He marshals four objections: settlement is distorted by disparities in the parties' resources; it founders on the problem of who can give authoritative consent for organizations and groups; it provides no basis for the continuing judicial involvement that structural-reform and other cases demand; and it deprives courts of the occasion to interpret public values and give force to law. Adjudication, on his account, is not merely private dispute resolution but a public exercise of state power in service of constitutional and statutory ideals.

item.title: Fuller - Forms and Limits of Adjudication
item.description: Lon L. Fuller — The Forms and Limits of Adjudication (92 Harv. L. Rev. 353 (1978))
item.content:encoded:
Lon L. Fuller — The Forms and Limits of Adjudication (92 Harv. L. Rev. 353 (1978))

Lon Fuller's classic essay analyzes adjudication as a distinct form of social ordering, defined by its characteristic mode of participation: the affected party participates by presenting proofs and reasoned arguments, in contrast to the negotiation of contract or the voting of elections. Fuller argues that this structure gives adjudication both its moral force and its intrinsic limits — it is well suited to yes-or-no questions of right and duty but ill suited to 'polycentric' problems, in which many interacting variables mean that resolving one issue unpredictably reshuffles all the others. Such tasks, he contends, are better handled by managerial direction or contract than by courts.

Chapters:
25:48 III. In discussing reciprocity and organization by common aims, I pointed out that th
1:17:19 Introduction
1:50:54 Introduction
2:23:47 Introduction

item.title: Chayes-RoleJudgePublic-1976
item.description: Abram Chayes — The Role of the Judge in Public Law Litigation (89 Harv. L. Rev. 1281 (1976))
item.content:encoded:
Abram Chayes — The Role of the Judge in Public Law Litigation (89 Harv. L. Rev. 1281 (1976))

Abram Chayes argues that the traditional model of civil adjudication — a bipolar, retrospective, party-controlled dispute over private rights — no longer captures much of what federal trial courts actually do. In its place he identifies an emerging model of 'public law litigation': sprawling, many-party suits seeking prospective, legislative-like injunctive relief, in which the judge becomes an active organizer and ongoing administrator of remedies that reach far beyond the named parties. Chayes contends that this expanded judicial role, though it raises real concerns of legitimacy, is workable and perhaps inevitable in a heavily regulated society.

Chapters:
5:40 I. The Received Tradition
13:36 II. The Public Law Litigation Model
42:57 III. A First APPRAISAL
1:04:36 IV. Some THOUGHTS ON LEGITIMACY Among the most important functions served by the tra

item.title: Marcus & Engstrom - Managerial Courts
item.description: Engstrom, Marcus & Setzer — Managerial Courts
item.content:encoded:
Engstrom, Marcus & Setzer — Managerial Courts

Engstrom, Marcus, and Setzer document the rise of "managerial courts" — state trial systems that have moved well beyond Resnik's "managerial judging" by reorganizing the work of adjudication itself through personnel reshuffling, systemic administration, and quality-control infrastructure (case-management teams, master calendars, bench cards, AI-assisted screening). They argue this transformation is largely invisible to legal scholarship, which remains fixated on federal-court managerial judging and on a Hart-Hand model of individuated, judge-centered decision-making that no longer describes how most American adjudication actually happens. The article then takes up how to optimize court managerialism — what to manage, how, and through which institutional commitments — without surrendering due process or the residual Hart-Hand judging that still grounds adjudicative legitimacy, treating quality control as the managerial mode most deserving of governance attention.

Chapters:
3:19:54 conclusion

item.title: Wu-Will_artificial_intelligence_eat_the_law_the_rise_of_hybrid_social_ordering_systems
item.description: Tim Wu — Will Artificial Intelligence Eat the Law? The Rise of Hybrid Social-Ordering Systems (119 Colum. L. Rev. 2001, 2019)
item.content:encoded:
Tim Wu — Will Artificial Intelligence Eat the Law? The Rise of Hybrid Social-Ordering Systems (119 Colum. L. Rev. 2001, 2019)

Tim Wu uses online speech moderation as a case study to argue that intelligent software will not, and should not, fully replace law and adjudication. Drawing on Facebook's and Twitter's shift away from "open and free" toward "healthy speech" environments, Wu shows how the major platforms have built hybrid social-ordering systems that combine algorithmic enforcement at scale with human reviewers and emerging court-like adjudicative bodies (such as Facebook's proposed "Supreme Court"). Code wins on speed, scale, and ex ante prevention, but it lacks the procedural fairness and contextual judgment that humans bring to hard cases. The right design aspiration, Wu argues, is the autopilot — not the self-driving car.

Chapters:
0:48 Introduction
10:44 I. Background
15:16 II. The Case Study: Facebook, Twitter, and Healthy Speech Environments
33:43 III. Hybrid Systems and the Comparative Advantages of Software and Courts
53:44 Conclusion

item.title: cheap
item.description: Eugene Volokh — Cheap Speech and What It Will Do
item.content:encoded:
Eugene Volokh — Cheap Speech and What It Will Do

Eugene Volokh argues that emerging digital-distribution technologies — the "information superhighway" — will dramatically reduce the cost of producing and distributing speech across music, books, magazines, newspapers, and video, breaking the power of publisher-intermediaries and shifting control to speakers and listeners. The resulting "cheap speech" environment will democratize the information marketplace, giving fringe voices access while letting listeners custom-tailor their media diets — outcomes that resemble the idealized open marketplace assumed by existing First Amendment doctrine. Volokh acknowledges a possible dark side (KKK-adjacent speakers also benefit, and the loss of a common mass-media baseline may fray social cohesion), but concludes the First Amendment will fit the new order better than it fit the old.

Chapters:
2:06 I. focus
28:19 Introduction
1:38:11 I. may be wrong in my predictions about what the new media order will

item.title: Volokh Lemley Henderson - 1A and AI Output
item.description: Volokh, Lemley & Henderson — Freedom of Speech and AI Output
item.content:encoded:
Volokh, Lemley & Henderson — Freedom of Speech and AI Output

Volokh, Lemley, and Henderson argue that the output of generative AI is likely entitled to First Amendment protection — not primarily because AI systems or their corporate creators are 'speakers,' but because users have independent constitutional rights to listen (to receive information and ideas) and to speak (using AI as a tool, much like a camera or printing press). The authors contend that even where the speaker-side rationale is murky, the listener-rights and user-speech rationales supply a firm basis for protection, analogous to the doctrines applied to foreign propagandists, corporate speech, and the writings of the long-dead.

item.title: 07_Kolt_GoverningAIAgents
item.description: Noam Kolt — Governing AI Agents (101 Notre Dame L. Rev., forthcoming)
item.content:encoded:
Noam Kolt — Governing AI Agents (101 Notre Dame L. Rev., forthcoming)

Noam Kolt argues that two long-established frameworks—the economic theory of principal-agent problems and the common law doctrine of agency relationships—offer powerful tools for governing AI agents, systems that autonomously plan and execute complex tasks on behalf of users. The Article uses agency law and theory to characterize the core problems AI agents raise (information asymmetry, discretionary authority, and loyalty), shows why conventional solutions like incentive design, monitoring, and enforcement may falter for agents that make uninterpretable decisions at unprecedented speed and scale, and contends that effective governance will require new technical and legal infrastructure built around principles of inclusivity, visibility, and liability.

Chapters:
1:37 Introduction
12:42 I. AI Agents
21:34 II. Evergreen Agency Problems
40:49 III. The Limits of Agency Law and Theory
52:48 IV. Implications for AI Design and Regulation
1:07:54 Conclusion

item.title: vol127_shaw
item.description: Geoffrey C. Shaw — H. L. A. Hart's Lost Essay: Discretion and the Legal Process School (127 Harv. L. Rev. 666, 2013)
item.content:encoded:
Geoffrey C. Shaw — H. L. A. Hart's Lost Essay: Discretion and the Legal Process School (127 Harv. L. Rev. 666, 2013)

Geoffrey Shaw analyzes a previously unpublished essay by H. L. A. Hart on discretion, written at Harvard Law School in November 1956 and rediscovered in the Harvard Law School Library. Hart argued that discretion is a special mode of reasoned, constrained decisionmaking that occupies a middle ground between arbitrary choice and determinate rule application, and that legal indeterminacy is fully compatible with the rule of law when judges resolve cases through rational principles. Shaw situates the essay within the Legal Process School and shows how it sheds light on Hart's later debates with Lon Fuller and Ronald Dworkin, revealing close intellectual connections between Hart's analytical positivism and the American process theorists at Harvard during his 1956–57 visit.

Chapters:
1:35 Introduction
20:37 I. Setting the Scene:
53:13 II. H
1:25:09 III. The Legacy of Discretion: the Harts, Fuller, and Dworkin

item.title: Arbel et al - AI A Corps
item.description: Arbel, Goldstein & Salib — How to Count AIs: Individuation and Liability for AI Agents
item.content:encoded:
Arbel, Goldstein & Salib — How to Count AIs: Individuation and Liability for AI Agents

This Article diagnoses the legal problem of identifying AI agents — both 'thinly' (tying AI actions to human principals for accountability) and 'thickly' (individuating AI agents as discrete entities with coherent goals that can be directly governed by law). The authors propose the 'Algorithmic Corporation' or A-corp, a legal-fictional entity owned by humans but run by AIs, which solves thin identity through ownership tracing and thick identity through emergent self-organization driven by resource constraints. The Article sketches implementation pathways and argues that A-corps would function as essential legal infrastructure for governing AI behavior in an economy populated by billions of autonomous agents.

Chapters:
2:30 Introduction
17:07 I. The Problem of AI Identity
58:12 II. The A-Corp: A Legal Solution to AI Identity
1:44:55 III. Implementation Pathways
1:57:43 IV. Objections and Responses
2:03:26 Conclusion

item.title: Schwarcz & Wolff - AI and Insurance
item.description: Schwarcz & Wolff — The Limits of Regulating AI Safety Through Liability and Insurance: Lessons from Cybersecurity
item.content:encoded:
Schwarcz & Wolff — The Limits of Regulating AI Safety Through Liability and Insurance: Lessons from Cybersecurity

Schwarcz and Wolff argue that proposals to regulate AI safety through tort liability and insurance are unlikely to succeed, drawing on two decades of failed efforts to regulate cybersecurity through similar mechanisms. Insurers have struggled to price cyber risk accurately or incentivize meaningful security improvements, and the authors contend that AI safety risks — characterized by opacity, rapid technological change, and lack of standardized loss data — pose even greater challenges. Rather than relying primarily on liability and insurance, the Article advocates for comprehensive ex ante regulation, beginning with mandatory, standardized collection and disclosure of AI safety data.

Chapters:
1:45 Introduction
11:10 I. Proposals to Regulate AI Safety Through Liability and Insurance
37:50 II. Failed Efforts to Regulate Cybersecurity Through Insurance
1:07:53 III. Lessons for AI from Cybersecurity: The Limits of Liability and
1:46:23 IV. Implications
1:58:51 Conclusion

item.title: Abraham_Self-Proving Causation
item.description: Kenneth S. Abraham — Self-Proving Causation (Virginia Law Review, 2013)
item.content:encoded:
Kenneth S. Abraham — Self-Proving Causation (Virginia Law Review, 2013)

Abraham explores a feature of causation doctrine he calls "self-proving causation" — situations where the very nature of a defendant's negligence provides circumstantial evidence that the negligence caused the plaintiff's harm. He analyzes the landmark Second Circuit decision in Zuchowicz v. United States, arguing that far from being a straightforward application of the doctrine, Zuchowicz extended it in ways that remain underappreciated, and examines what the case may mean for the future of causation law.

Chapters:
6:25 I. The Nature of Cause-In-Fact and the Distinctiveness of Self-Proving Causation
37:25 II. Zuchowicz
56:09 III. A New Law of Self-Proving Causation?
1:37:00 Conclusion

item.title: facct2025-final2475-acmpaginated
item.description: Shira Gur-Arieh and Christina Lee — Consistently Arbitrary or Arbitrarily Consistent: Navigating the Tensions Between Homogenization and Multiplicity in Algorithmic Decision-Making (FAccT 2025)
item.content:encoded:
Shira Gur-Arieh and Christina Lee — Consistently Arbitrary or Arbitrarily Consistent: Navigating the Tensions Between Homogenization and Multiplicity in Algorithmic Decision-Making (FAccT 2025)

Gur-Arieh and Lee argue that two well-known critiques of algorithmic decision-making — outcome homogenization from consistency at scale, and arbitrariness from predictive multiplicity — are not separate problems but two faces of the same trade-off, with most fixes to one quietly making the other worse. The paper proposes a framework that defaults to diversity across an ecosystem of decision-makers but determinacy within any single one, and offers four factors (normative-vs-factual, uncertainty, single- vs. multi-shot, public vs. private) for deciding when ecosystem-level uniformity is actually warranted.

item.title: Fahey
item.description: Fahey — AI as Administrative Tool (workshop draft)
item.content:encoded:
Fahey — AI as Administrative Tool (workshop draft)

This early-stage workshop draft argues that agencies adopting AI tools are not operating in a legal vacuum: existing statutory delegations and constitutional authorities already supply ample authorization for the government to build and deploy AI in its administrative work, from congressional grants like the Stafford and Post-Katrina emergency-management statutes to the President's executive authority. Fahey's worry is the flip side of that abundance—an 'authorization anachronism' problem in which old statutes, written for an earlier technological era, are stretched to license powerful new AI-powered capabilities, eroding the democratic assumptions and built-in technological limits that once constrained what agencies could do. The draft sketches how AI alters the relationship between substantive authority and the practical capacities that shape what the administrative state can actually accomplish.

Chapters:
0:03 Introduction
7:09 I. A Basic Sketch of AI’s Authorization
19:57 II. Implications
32:11 Conclusion

item.title: Cohen_Administrative_Legitimacy
item.description: Julie E. Cohen — Administrative Legitimacy in the Age of Automation (workshop draft)
item.content:encoded:
Julie E. Cohen — Administrative Legitimacy in the Age of Automation (workshop draft)

In this rough workshop draft, Julie Cohen argues that rule-of-law objections to automated administrative decision-making rest on a classic paradigm—built around legislatures and courts—that bureaucracies never fit in the first place. Rather than treating administrative automation as presumptively illegitimate, she contends that bureaucracies have distinctive strengths (operating at and across scales, synthesizing diverse evidence, iterating over time, and generating recursive internal knowledge) that a networked information economy requires, and she sketches a "fit for purpose" rule-of-law framework—grounded in tempering arbitrary power and an equality principle, and structured by transparency, care and respect, and oversight—for judging when administrative automation is legitimate.

Chapters:
4:04 I. The Algocrat in the Infrastructure
8:53 II. The Bureaucrat in the Attic Critics of incipient algocracy in administrative gov
14:36 III. The Underappreciated Virtues of Bureaucracy
27:38 IV. Toward a Rule-of-Law Framework for Administrative Governance So far, I have esta
40:31 V. Automation and Administrative Legitimacy, Reconsidered Armed with a coherent acc
43:05 Conclusion
